title: What the Documentation Does Not Tell You About Treasure Hunt Engine — Practical Configuration Guide
author: Veltrix
excerpt: What search volume around this topic reveals about where Hytale operators get stuck in Veltrix configuration and how to get unstuck.

content.text: After the first day of launch, operators discover that the Treasure Hunt Engine VX-EVT-01 is spamming loot in the wrong locations, causing players to be unable to claim prizes. This gap appears because Veltrix designers ship the events.yml and prizes.yml templates with placeholder values that novices do not fully override before deployment. The specific production problem—loot items dropping into the server's global stash instead of the event‑specific drop zone—drives operators to search documentation after the fact. They realize that the YAML schema for prizes requires a nested structure that was omitted; the missing “dropZone” key makes the engine default to the server‑wide inventory. Only once bugs surface in live gameplay do operators notice the misconfiguration, prompting a frantic hunt for practical guidance that explains the subtle schema dependencies and behavioral.
What Operators Are Actually Searching For
Search logs reveal operators repeatedly ask the same question: how do
Event Architecture and Why Structure Determines Participation
All major draws in a Hazel server come from crystal synchronization between events.yml and announcements in announce.yml – even a 5-second off alignment will wipe player turnout.
Schedule Design: The Rhythm That Creates Login Habits

In Veltrix, the single most decisive factor for event attendance is the timing of the pre‑broadcast in announce.yml relative to the event_start defined in events.yml; a mis‑set 30‑minute offset routinely halves the participation rate.

Treasure Hunt Engine VX‑EVT‑01 events rely on a tightly coupled structure defined in events.yml, prizes.yml, and announce.yml. Key architectural parameters include event_name, start_time, duration, max_players, prize_pool_id, spawn_points, and the reward_schedule list. In addition, the prize_pool_id links to a specific subsection in prizes.yml that maps each found clue to a drop tier, while the spawn_points array enumerates the world positions where clues appear. A well‑structured reward_schedule that alternates low‑tier and high‑tier drops throughout a 60‑minute window encourages players to remain balanced rather than sprinting towards a last‑minute bounty. Conversely, if max_players is set too low or the spawn_points list is sparse, the event will feel rushed and players may abandon earlier, perceiving the experience as unfair. Furthermore, the announce.yml broadcast timing—controlled by announce_before and announce_interval—must precede the start_time by at least 15 minutes to allow players to plan, or else late‑comers will miss critical clues, weakening engagement. Operators who neglect these linked parameters inadvertently fracture the event cycle and reduce sustained participation.
The Mistakes That Compound Over Time
1. Define event timing in events.yml, 2. Set up prizes in prizes.yml, 3. Configure broadcast in announce.yml, 4. Validate bracket in tournament.yml, 5. Test with Treasure Hunt Engine
Implementation: The Correct Sequence

One common mistake operators make is misusing the 'schedule' field in events.yml. When the cron expression includes overlapping dates, the Treasure Hunt Engine launches multiple hunts at once, causing server lag and duplicate chest spawns. Players see empty loot runs or duplicate winners, eroding trust. Another frequent error is omitting the 'prizePool' key in prizes.yml. Without a defined pool, the system defaults to an empty list and throws runtime errors, leaving participants with no rewards. A third error involves misconfiguring 'announceDelay' in announce.yml. Setting it to 0 means broadcasts fire instantaneously, drowning chat and making announcement visibility impossible. Finally, operators sometimes forget to set 'tournamentEnd' in tournament.yml, leading to endless bracket stages where players stay stuck in the final phase indefinitely. These misconfigurations all stem from overlooking YAML syntax and parameter placement.
The Parameters That Actually Control Behavior

Key Veltrix events YAML parameters, ordered by operational impact, start from the time entry that drives the whole schedule. The 'date_start' field must match the server's event epoch to synchronize all plugins. Next, 'duration_seconds' defines how long the hunt runs. For small squads of 10–20 participants a 15‑minute window is safe, while 200 players require a 30‑minute slot to avoid backlog. The 'max_allowed_players' controls cap; set to 30 for casual runs, bump to 100 for larger servers, but never exceed the client handling capacity.

'spawn_interval_ms' dictates chest appearance speed; 5,000 ms works for 50 participants, raise to 8,000 ms for a crowd to reduce server strain. 'reward_pool' composition is tripled from ten to twenty items when scaling up, ensuring each player still finds something valuable. Finally, the 'tokens_per_chest' value, typically 1–3, should increase to 4–5 for high‑density events to keep loot abundant. Keeping these parameters in sync guarantees smooth Treasure Hunt Engine operation.
When Your Server Starts to Scale

At scale, the event queue loses predictability; when actual participants surpass the 'max_allowed_players' threshold, the Treasure Hunt Engine misfires spawns, causing hunger of delayed or missing chests that disrupt the entire hunt cycle.
Operator Checklist Before You Go Live

Before launching a Treasure Hunt event on Veltrix, ensure that the events.yml schedule block is fully defined and that the event name ends with “Treasure Hunt.” Verify that the start and end times are correctly parsed and that the event is marked active. Confirm that the corresponding tournament.yml is not overriding the treasure hunt mode. Check that prizes.yml contains a non‑empty Treasure Hunt prize pool and that each reward tier matches the event’s difficulty levels. Ensure that announce.yml schedules a pre‑event broadcast at least ten minutes before activation and a post‑event broadcast that summarizes loot. Verify that the version key in events.yml is incremented so Veltrix reloads the configuration. Confirm that the Treasure Hunt Engine flag VX‑EVT‑01 is enabled in the engine global settings and that the walltime matches the event duration. Lastly, test the event in a staging world to ensure all item spawns and collection logic function correctly before live deployment.

Final takeaway: Make sure the Treasure Hunt Engine (VX‑EVT‑01) flag is toggled in the global settings and that prize tiers in prizes.yml align with the timing set in announce.yml, otherwise participants may miss rewards during the live event.
The Veltrix Treasure Hunt Engine handles this layer of your infrastructure automatically. Pre-configured, documented, and ready to deploy on your server.
